result:
The first thing that strikes you, when you step off the train and the doors sigh shut behind you, is the smell of wet limestone. It is Monday everywhere else on the planet, but here it is simply the day after the mist arrived. The station clock has stopped at 3:17, and everyone treats it as the true time, dragging shadows that lie short and sideways like pets unwilling to leave the square. A woman hands you a card that reads: Speak only in questions until you hear a bell. There is no bell visible.

You pocket the card without comment and follow the sloping path into town. Roofs are made of rusted piano lids; chords rattle when the wind is right. Children chase soap bubbles the size of moons; each bubble contains the hushed silhouette of a city yet unbuilt, cranes and rivers flickering inside them like goldfish learning geography. When a bubble pops, someone new is born—fully grown, already nostalgic. One stands beside you in a coat stitched from lost puzzles. “Tell me something I haven’t forgotten,” she pleads, but the rules forbid an answer until the bell; so you uncup your hands and show her the echo of a train whistle that flew in with you.

The pavement begins to slope upward for no architectural reason. Gravity, like metaphor, has moods here. Streetlamps unwrap themselves from the fog, skinny monks lighting one another’s cigarettes. You pass a bookstore titled The Map Is Later, shelves crammed with dictionaries of silence. The owner—fox-eyed, ink on his breath—offers a pencil: soft lead, smells of cedar and thunderstorm. “Lines remember what color forgets,” he whispers, clearly violating the card, but no one arrests him. You purchase a sentence that ends with an ellipsis and place it in your pocket beside the card; together they rustle like moths.

Outside, the town has rearranged itself: the fountain now stands on the courthouse steps; pigeons perch on gushing stone tongues. A man with a telescope for an eye insists the sky is only wallpaper. He peels a corner to show the brick underneath, mortar still fresh. As he reaches to show you more, a girl chalks the word “Stop” across the courtyard, and everyone does, even the air. Out of the hush emerges a cart selling liquid afternoons—amber, stirred by an old woman who hums the exact tune your mother forgot between verses of lullabies. Time is served in paper cups; you sip 2:43 p.m. and taste the conversation you meant to have at sixteen.

From the cup slips a drop that turns into a sparrow. It flits to your shoulder, whispers that the bell is hidden inside a question no one has asked yet. Encouraged, you re-enter the bookstore and ask: “What becomes of the stories we never tell?” The shelves ripple like pond water; a single volume flops open to reveal cuffs and ankles of sleeping words waiting to be read so they can finish their dreams. The fox-eyed owner bows. “That was question three-hundred and twenty-six. Five more and the bell will borrow your tongue.”

So you move through the question-marked afternoon. In the arcade of locksmiths, you ask: “What key fits tomorrow?” They all point to the one marked with teeth shaped like regrets. Next, the tailor who stitches with night fabric shows you a coat that keeps in everything except longing; you ask: “Is there enough of it left?” Each yes feels like verdicts pronounced by clocks. At the edge of the market, a man balances on a ladder made of exclamation points repainted into curves; he is hanging moons the way others hang lanterns. You ask: “What phase reminds us to begin?” He offers a crescent whisper: “Whatever is missing.”

Question five you speak aloud into the postbox that speaks back only with stamps licked by ghosts: “If every exile carries a missing bell, which is the country that rings us home?” A small brass tongue rattles in the envelope slot; it is warm, insists on being carried under the tongue. For the final question you hesitate—you have only one heartbeat left before syntactical bankruptcy—so you ask the sparrow on your shoulder: “Who here is dreaming me?” At that, the world performs an audible hush, the kind heard when cinemas stop mid-reel and the theater discovers it is actually inside the film.

The brass tongue rings against your teeth—a bell small enough to lose, loud enough to wake the town from grammar. Everyone turns expectantly. You are now free to speak flat declarations, but everything tastes of lime and unfinished lullabies; questions feel more honest. You open your mouth; instead of answer or question, you release a color that has been hiding between paragraphs of the sky. It unfurls into a sail stitched from every second you almost believed lost: the hour your father taught you torque by opening a jar of pickles, the minute the ambulance took another route, the second you forgave yourself for not becoming a lighthouse.

The sail catches the upturned faces like breezes catch windows. It lifts you two inches off the ground—no more; levitation is regulated—but sufficient to see rooftops as they really are: ribs of massive animals we’ve been living inside all along, breathing through our chimneys. At this height the town looks like one sentence still being written across the valley; the mist is the pen pausing. The fox-eyed owner waves his cedar pencil; every chimney sketches its own ending in smoke.

An echo arrives—footsteps made of cathedral stone. Between beats strides the woman in the coat of puzzles. Now the missing pieces have been stitched in place, pictures joined so that her lapels display the complete map of the place you were trying to leave but were really circling. She smiles with the crooked geometry of solved riddles. “You could have spoken at any time,” she says, not unkindly. “Every question contains its own bell.”

She removes a single jigsaw piece from her cuff, offers it over. On its lacquered surface is an image of you, but flipped, right-handed instead of left, holding something dark and shining. You take it. Around you, the townspeople begin to hum—not the words, but the pauses between them. They are composing the quiet after the last question, the hush that follows any good story. You would like to thank them, but gratitude feels too much like an exclamation, and the gentle mood prefers commas, ellipses, parentheses curved like arms holding everything lightly.

So instead you place the puzzle piece on your tongue like a wafer, and the bell changes key. Down you drift, the sail folding back into your voice. When your shoes touch cobblestone, the entire pavement rolls itself into scrolls and tucks under your arm: destiny reduced to a commuting paper. The sparrow, now silent, flutters ahead into the station where the clock still says 3:17, but its hands have become compasses. You check your pockets: card gone, echo gone, only the unfinished sentence remains—without its ellipsis. The final dot has alighted on your wrist in the shape of a freckle; time begins again where moons once hung.

The train arrives with doors already open as if it has been waiting for an apology. You step inside and discover all the seats filled with books the fox-eyed man never sold, pages turning themselves in a pleased wind. As the train pulls away, the town folds like a letter addressed to who you used to be; you watch through lit windows while the wet-limestone smell is replaced by engine steam. Somewhere among the carriages rings the faintest echo of a bell you now realize has always been your own heart rattling inside its bone chapel. At exactly 3:17, the clock starts, and you finish the sentence you once began so long ago outside language: “Perhaps the journey is only the sound a story makes when walking home.”

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
